name: Fort Duquesne Bridge
funFact: This massive bridge carries six lanes of highway traffic and connects downtown Pittsburgh to the North Shore — home of PNC Park and Acrisure Stadium. It's one of the longest cantilever truss bridges in Pennsylvania!

name: Roberto Clemente Bridge
funFact: On Pittsburgh Pirates home game days, this bridge closes to cars so fans can walk across! It's named for Pirates legend Roberto Clemente, who was famous for both his amazing baseball skills and his kind heart helping others.

name: Andy Warhol Bridge
funFact: Named for Pittsburgh-born pop artist Andy Warhol! In 2009, a group of knitters covered this entire bridge in colorful yarn — a craft called 'yarn bombing' — to celebrate its 83rd birthday. It took 10,000 hours!

name: Rachel Carson Bridge
funFact: This bridge and its two sisters (the Clemente and Warhol bridges) are the only set of three identical self-anchored suspension bridges in the entire world! It's named for Rachel Carson, a western Pennsylvania scientist who helped start the modern environmental movement.

name: David McCullough Bridge
funFact: Named for historian David McCullough, who grew up in Point Breeze, Pittsburgh. Look closely at the ornate towers — they're decorated with beautiful sculpted eagles, shields, and intricate metalwork that took skilled craftsmen years to create!

name: 31st Street Bridge
funFact: This classic truss bridge connects the Strip District to the Deutschtown neighborhood on the North Side, one of Pittsburgh's oldest neighborhoods settled by German immigrants in the 1800s.

name: 40th Street Bridge
funFact: Built during the Roaring Twenties, this bridge connects the hip Lawrenceville neighborhood to the Borough of Millvale. From the middle of the bridge you can see Pittsburgh's hillside neighborhoods climbing steeply away from the river in both directions!

name: 62nd Street Bridge
funFact: This bridge has been connecting Morningside on the Pittsburgh side to Millvale on the north bank since 1927. Millvale is famous for its incredible murals painted inside Saint Nicholas Croatian Catholic Church by artist Maxo Vanka.

name: Highland Park Bridge
funFact: This arch bridge is right next to the Pittsburgh Zoo and PPG Aquarium! The zoo opened in 1898 and today is home to over 4,000 animals. Cross this bridge and you might hear the lions roar!

name: Washington Crossing Bridge
funFact: This bridge is named in honor of George Washington, who crossed the Allegheny River near this area in 1753 on a dangerous winter mission during the French and Indian War — when he was just 21 years old!

name: Hulton Bridge
funFact: Just a mile from this bridge sits the famous Oakmont Country Club, which has hosted more major golf championships than any other course in America! Presidents, celebrities, and the world's best golfers have played there.

name: Fort Pitt Bridge
funFact: Drivers coming out of the Fort Pitt Tunnel get Pittsburgh's most famous surprise — the entire downtown skyline suddenly appears! This magical 'Pittsburgh revealed' moment is considered one of the best highway entrances to any city in the world.

name: Smithfield Street Bridge
funFact: Built in 1883, this is Pittsburgh's oldest bridge still open to traffic! Its lenticular (lens-shaped) truss design was an engineering marvel of its time. The ornate lamp posts and metalwork make it one of the most beautiful bridges in the city.

name: Liberty Bridge
funFact: One of the busiest bridges in all of Pittsburgh — tens of thousands of cars cross Liberty Bridge every single day! It carries people between downtown and the South Hills neighborhoods, and runs right next to the Liberty Tubes tunnels.

name: Birmingham Bridge
funFact: Pittsburgh's only cable-stayed bridge over a major river! You can recognize it instantly by its tall towers with cables fanning out like a giant harp. It was named for the old Birmingham neighborhood, now part of the South Side.

name: Hot Metal Bridge
funFact: This bridge gets its awesome name from its original job — it carried ladles of liquid hot molten steel across the river between steel mills! Now it's a peaceful trail for walkers and bikers, part of the Great Allegheny Passage trail system.

name: Glenwood Bridge
funFact: This bridge carries I-837, which connects the Mon Valley communities to Pittsburgh. From its deck, you can see miles of the Monongahela River winding through the valley where Pittsburgh's legendary steel industry once roared day and night.

name: Homestead Grays Bridge
carries: US-30 / Homestead High Level Bridge Road
funFact: Named for the Homestead Grays, one of the greatest teams in Negro League baseball history! Slugger Josh Gibson — sometimes called the 'Black Babe Ruth' — was their star player. Below this bridge once stood the massive Homestead Steel Works, one of the world's most famous steel mills.

name: Rankin Bridge
funFact: This bridge carries the historic Lincoln Highway (US-30), one of the very first coast-to-coast roads in America! Built in 1913, the Lincoln Highway stretched from New York City all the way to San Francisco — a 3,400-mile adventure!

name: McKeesport–Duquesne Bridge
funFact: McKeesport was once called 'Tube City' because it was the center of America's steel pipe and tube manufacturing — the pipes that carry water and gas to homes across the country were made right here in this Mon Valley town!

name: West End Bridge
funFact: Completed in 1932, the same year as the famous Sydney Harbour Bridge in Australia! At night, the West End Bridge is beautifully illuminated and reflects in the Ohio River. It marks the spot where Pittsburgh's three rivers meet the Ohio and flow all the way to the Mississippi!

name: McKees Rocks Bridge
funFact: Right nearby is the McKees Rocks Mounds — a prehistoric burial site built by Native Americans over 2,000 years ago! The people who built these mounds are known as the Adena culture, and their earthworks are some of the oldest structures in western Pennsylvania.

name: Coraopolis Bridge
funFact: Coraopolis comes from the Greek word for 'maiden city.' Just a few miles from this bridge is Pittsburgh International Airport — so cross this bridge and you might look up and see planes coming in to land overhead!

name: Sewickley Bridge
funFact: The Borough of Sewickley is named after a Lenape (Delaware) word believed to mean 'sweet water.' It's one of Pittsburgh's most historic river towns, and the view of the Ohio River from this bridge is one of the prettiest in the entire region!

name: Fort Wayne Railroad Bridge
carries: Norfolk Southern railroad tracks and lower-deck pedestrian/bicycle trail
funFact: This bridge has an active upper railroad deck and a lower deck used by trail users to cross the Allegheny near Point State Park.

name: Neville Island Bridge
funFact: Neville Island is one of the few populated islands in western Pennsylvania, and this bridge is your gateway onto it — the island is home to large industrial facilities and a small historic community that dates back to the 1800s!

name: Greenfield Bridge
funFact: The original 1921 bridge here was so deteriorated that chunks of concrete fell onto the highway traffic below — PennDOT rebuilt it in 2017 but kept the original stone abutments to preserve a piece of Pittsburgh history!

name: Fern Hollow Bridge
funFact: On January 28, 2022 — the exact morning President Biden flew to Pittsburgh to speak about infrastructure — this bridge suddenly collapsed and made national news! The city rebuilt it in a record 11 months.

name: Bloomfield Bridge
funFact: Standing on this bridge you can look straight down nearly 100 feet to railroad tracks below — it's one of the most dramatic street bridges in Pittsburgh and connects the lively Bloomfield neighborhood to artsy Polish Hill!

name: Panther Hollow Bridge
funFact: This beautiful stone arch bridge has been winding through Schenley Park since 1897, making it one of Pittsburgh's oldest bridges — it was built before the Wright Brothers ever flew an airplane!

name: Junction Hollow Bridge
funFact: This bridge carries the Boulevard of the Allies over the wooded Junction Hollow valley in Schenley Park — on foggy mornings the treetops below look like a secret forest floating in the mist!

name: Larimer Avenue Bridge
funFact: The Larimer neighborhood sits on a hillside above a major railroad corridor, and this bridge carries the avenue over tracks that have been rumbling with freight trains for over 150 years!

name: Spring Garden Bridge
funFact: Spring Garden was one of Pittsburgh's first suburban neighborhoods, developed in the 1800s when residents rode horse-drawn streetcars across the Allegheny River to escape the city's industrial smoke!

name: Route 28 Viaduct
funFact: Route 28 hugs the Allegheny River valley as it heads north from Pittsburgh — from this elevated section you can see the same river corridor that Native Americans, French and British soldiers, and American settlers all used as their main highway through western Pennsylvania!

name: Second Avenue Viaduct
funFact: Second Avenue passes through Hazelwood, one of Pittsburgh's most dramatically changing neighborhoods — the massive former steel mill site visible nearby is now being transformed into Hazelwood Green, a major new tech and research campus!

name: Chartiers Creek Bridge
funFact: Chartiers Creek has one of western Pennsylvania's most unusual pronunciations — locals say 'SHAT-ers Creek,' a name that traces back through French colonial maps to a Lenape Native American word from centuries ago!

name: Herron Avenue Bridge
funFact: This bridge spans the old Pennsylvania Railroad cut that sliced through Pittsburgh's East End in the 1800s — a reminder of when the railroads were so powerful they could literally divide an entire city in half!

name: Negley Avenue Bridge
funFact: Negley Avenue is named for the Negley family, one of Pittsburgh's pioneering families who owned much of the East End land — their farm was eventually subdivided into the neighborhoods of Shadyside and East Liberty!

name: Penn Avenue Viaduct
funFact: Penn Avenue was Pittsburgh's original Main Street, stretching from downtown all the way through the East End — this viaduct elevated it above the railroad corridor that once separated East Liberty from the rest of the city!

name: Girty's Run Bridge
funFact: Girty's Run is named after Simon Girty, a famous frontier scout of the American Revolution era who served as an interpreter between European settlers and Native American nations along the Ohio River in the 1770s!

name: Banksville Road Bridge
funFact: Route 19 is one of the longest state routes in Pennsylvania, stretching over 300 miles from the West Virginia border all the way north to Lake Erie — and this overpass is just one small link in that epic chain!

name: Baum Boulevard Bridge
crosses: Penn Central / Norfolk Southern railroad cut
funFact: The railroad cut under this bridge was carved through Pittsburgh's East End hillside by the Pennsylvania Railroad in the 1800s — today the same corridor carries freight trains and forms a natural wildlife greenway threading between Bloomfield and Shadyside!

name: New Fern Hollow Bridge
funFact: Drivers entering the Squirrel Hill Tunnels pass directly under this bridge — the twin tunnels bore 1,100 feet straight through the hillside, making the entire Squirrel Hill neighborhood literally the mountain overhead!

name: Murray Avenue Bridge
funFact: Murray Avenue is the heart of Pittsburgh's vibrant Jewish community — the bakeries, delis, and shops just steps from this overpass have fed Pittsburgh families for generations, making Squirrel Hill one of the most celebrated neighborhoods in the whole city!

name: Washington Boulevard Viaduct
crosses: Penn Central railroad cut / East Busway
funFact: The railroad cut running below this viaduct was converted into Pittsburgh's East Busway in 1983 — one of America's first dedicated bus rapid transit lanes, which still saves thousands of commuters hours of travel time every week!

name: West Liberty Avenue Bridge
crosses: Saw Mill Run / light rail tracks
funFact: The light rail (T) tracks running through this valley follow the ancient path of Saw Mill Run Creek — the same route Pittsburgh's earliest settlers used as a wagon road to reach their farms south of the city in the 1700s!

name: Saw Mill Run Boulevard Bridge
funFact: Saw Mill Run Creek was named for the water-powered sawmills that settlers built along its banks in the 1700s to cut lumber for Pittsburgh's first homes — the creek still flows beneath this bridge on its way to the Ohio River, just as it has for thousands of years!

name: Frick Park Footbridge
funFact: Nine Mile Run was once buried under a massive pile of steel-mill slag (waste), but a landmark cleanup project in the 1990s and 2000s restored it to a natural stream flowing through 644-acre Frick Park — one of the biggest urban stream restorations ever completed in the US!

name: Browns Hill Road Bridge
funFact: Browns Hill Road winds along the wooded border of Frick Park — the park was donated to Pittsburgh by Henry Clay Frick, Andrew Carnegie's business partner, and at 644 acres it remains one of the largest urban parks in the entire United States!

name: Schenley Drive Bridge
funFact: Pittsburgh saved Schenley Park in 1889 by sending a telegram to landowner Mary Schenley in England just before a developer could buy her 300 acres — she donated the land instead, and today this stone arch bridge is one of the oldest structures still standing inside the park!

name: Heth's Run Bridge
funFact: Butler Street is Lawrenceville's main avenue, lined with the galleries, breweries, and boutiques that helped transform this former industrial neighborhood into one of Pittsburgh's most celebrated destinations — while the railroad that built the neighborhood still rumbles beneath this viaduct every day!

name: East Ohio Street Bridge
funFact: East Ohio Street runs through Deutschtown — Pittsburgh's historic German neighborhood where 19th-century immigrants established breweries, butcher shops, and lively beer gardens, and 'Deutsch' is simply the German word for German!

name: Marshall Avenue Bridge
funFact: Marshall-Shadeland on Pittsburgh's far North Side was laid out in the early 1900s with wide boulevards, envisioned as a grand suburban community — today it is one of the most spacious and tree-lined neighborhoods on the North Side!

name: Braddock Avenue Bridge
funFact: Braddock Avenue is named for General Edward Braddock, the British commander whose force was ambushed near here in 1755 during the French and Indian War — a young George Washington was Braddock's aide and narrowly escaped with his life in that famous defeat!

name: Perrysville Avenue Bridge
funFact: Perrysville Avenue is named for Commodore Oliver Hazard Perry, the naval hero who defeated the British at the Battle of Lake Erie in 1813 — his victory message 'We have met the enemy and they are ours' became one of the most famous lines in American military history!

name: I-579 Viaduct
crosses: railroad corridor / Lower Hill District valley
funFact: I-579 is one of the shortest Interstate highways in America at just over a mile long, but even this tiny expressway needs bridges to cross the deep valleys and railroad corridors that cut through the heart of Pittsburgh!

name: McArdle Roadway Bridge
funFact: McArdle Roadway twists up the steep face of Mt. Washington connecting the South Side to the hilltop neighborhood — at the bottom, this bridge carries the road over the old railroad tracks that once served the steel mills lining the Monongahela River!

name: Brownsville Road Viaduct
funFact: Brownsville Road has been one of Pittsburgh's main southside routes for over 200 years — the same road that once carried pioneer settlers in wagons toward the frontier now carries thousands of cars past Carrick's neighborhood shops every single day!

name: Lincoln Avenue Bridge
funFact: Bellevue's Lincoln Avenue is named for President Abraham Lincoln, and this bridge crosses over the railroad corridor that helped fuel Pittsburgh's explosive industrial growth during and after the Civil War — the same tracks carried coal, iron ore, and finished steel products across the country!

name: Grant Avenue Bridge
funFact: Millvale sits in a narrow valley carved by Girty's Run Creek, and this bridge carries you over the same waterway that frontier scout Simon Girty followed as his wilderness path through western Pennsylvania in the 1770s!

name: Butler Street Bridge
funFact: Etna Borough is named after Mount Etna, the famous active volcano in Sicily, Italy — early settlers gave it that dramatic name because the iron forges and furnaces here belched so much fire and smoke it reminded them of a real volcano!

name: Centre Avenue Bridge
funFact: The Hill District is the historic heart of Pittsburgh's African American community and the birthplace of jazz legend Ahmad Jamal and playwright August Wilson — the neighborhood's music and culture have influenced artists all around the world!

name: Schenley Park Bridle Path Bridge
funFact: Before cars existed, Pittsburgh's residents rode horses through Schenley Park on these bridle trails — the stone bridges were built so horses could safely cross the rocky streams and ravines on their afternoon rides through the 456-acre park!

name: Frick Park Glen Trail Bridge
funFact: The Glen Trail through Frick Park winds along Nine Mile Run through a surprisingly wild forest right inside the city — it's one of Pittsburgh's favorite spots to hear birds, spot deer, and feel like you've escaped into the wilderness without leaving the neighborhood!

name: Shaler Street Bridge
funFact: Girty's Run flows through the narrow valley between Etna and Sharpsburg and empties into the Allegheny River nearby — the creek and the borough of Shaler Township that borders this area are both named after legendary frontier figures from Pennsylvania's earliest history!

name: South 10th Street Bridge
funFact: The South Side Flats was laid out in 1811 by city planner Nathaniel Bedford, and its numbered streets — 1st through 22nd — still follow his original grid plan along the Monongahela River bank, making it one of Pittsburgh's most logical neighborhoods to navigate!

name: South Highland Avenue Bridge
funFact: Shadyside was developed in the 1880s as an upper-class suburb, and the railroad corridor under this bridge was the very reason wealthy Pittsburghers could live here — a short commute by steam train brought them downtown to manage their steel and glass empires!

name: East Side Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: East Liberty was once called Pittsburgh's 'second downtown' with thriving department stores and movie theaters, but urban renewal in the 1960s dramatically reshaped it — today it's one of Pittsburgh's tech hubs, home to Google's Pittsburgh office and dozens of restaurants!

name: Busway Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: This bridge sits right on the border between two of Pittsburgh's most famous neighborhoods — Shadyside to the south with its Victorian homes and boutiques, and East Liberty to the north, where you'll find Google's Pittsburgh office in a converted Nabisco factory!

name: South Graham Street Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: Shadyside's Walnut Street is just steps away from this bridge — for over a century it has been one of Pittsburgh's premier shopping destinations, and the beautiful brick storefronts lining the street today look remarkably similar to how they did when they were first built in the 1890s!

name: South Aiken Avenue Bridge
funFact: The railroad corridor below this bridge once connected Pittsburgh to Philadelphia and beyond — in the 1800s, this same rail line carried iron and coal that fueled the industrial revolution, and today it still carries freight trains loaded with goods heading east across Pennsylvania!

name: South Millvale Avenue Bridge
funFact: Bloomfield is Pittsburgh's 'Little Italy,' settled by Italian immigrants in the early 1900s who built the bakeries, delis, and restaurants that still define the neighborhood — Penn Avenue nearby remains one of Pittsburgh's most vibrant commercial corridors with food from around the world!

name: 28th Street Bridge
funFact: The Strip District stretches along the Allegheny River between downtown and Lawrenceville and was once Pittsburgh's wholesale produce and food district — today it's a food lover's paradise packed with specialty grocers, bakeries, and restaurants that attract visitors from all over the region!

name: North Lang Avenue Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: Point Breeze North sits adjacent to Frick Pittsburgh, the estate of steel magnate Henry Clay Frick — his mansion Clayton, built in 1882, still stands nearby as a beautifully preserved window into the Gilded Age when Pittsburgh's industrial titans lived like royalty!

name: Bajo Street Bridge
carries: Bajo Street off Becks Run Road
funFact: Becks Run Road winds along one of Pittsburgh's many steep creek hollows, and the stream below this bridge flows all the way down to the Monongahela River — Pittsburgh has over 90 named streams and waterways, more than almost any other American city of its size!

name: West Ohio Street Bridge
funFact: Deutschtown is one of Pittsburgh's oldest neighborhoods, settled by German immigrants in the 1830s who built beer gardens, churches, and brick rowhouses — today it's experiencing a major renaissance and was named one of the coolest neighborhoods in America by several national publications!

name: E North Avenue Bridge
funFact: North Avenue was the main commercial street of Allegheny City before it was controversially annexed into Pittsburgh in 1907 — residents of Allegheny City voted against the merger multiple times, but the Pennsylvania legislature approved it anyway, making it one of the most contested municipal mergers in American history!

name: Lowrie Street Bridge
funFact: Millvale is squeezed into a narrow valley between steep hillsides along Girty's Run Creek, making bridges absolutely essential for getting around — the borough has more bridge crossings per square mile than almost any community in western Pennsylvania!

name: Maple Avenue Bridge
funFact: Fineview is one of Pittsburgh's smallest hilltop neighborhoods, perched high above the Ohio and Allegheny Rivers on the North Side — from Fineview's hilltop streets on a clear day you can spot the famous confluence of Pittsburgh's three rivers all at once!

name: Milroy Street Bridge
funFact: East Allegheny, also called Deutschtown, was settled by German immigrants followed by Polish and Italian families — the neighborhood's Federal-style brick rowhouses from the 1800s are among the best-preserved in all of Pittsburgh and draw architecture lovers from across the region!

name: Columbus Avenue Bridge
funFact: California Avenue is one of the North Side's main commercial corridors, and the California-Kirkbride neighborhood was originally developed in the late 1800s as a streetcar suburb — the trolley tracks are long gone but the neighborhood's Victorian architecture still gives it a charming early-20th-century feel!

name: Pennsylvania Avenue Bridge
funFact: Allegheny West is one of Pittsburgh's most architecturally stunning neighborhoods, with beautifully restored Victorian mansions that once housed Pittsburgh's industrial elite — it was added to the National Register of Historic Places and sits next to the famous Allegheny Observatory!

name: Meadow Street Bridge
funFact: Heth's Run carved a deep ravine through the hillside between Lawrenceville and Stanton Heights — the stream was named for an early American officer, and the steep valley it created made bridges like this one essential for connecting Pittsburgh's hillside neighborhoods to the flats below!

name: Crane Avenue Bridge
funFact: Allentown sits on a high ridge overlooking the South Side and was home to Pittsburgh's last natural lake — Peerless Lake — which was filled in during the early 1900s, and today Allentown's hilltop location gives it some of the most dramatic views of Pittsburgh's downtown skyline!

name: Lowe Street Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: The West End sits where Chartiers Creek meets the Ohio River, and the name 'Chartiers' comes from French-Canadian trader Peter Chartier who canoed these waters in the 1720s — long before Pittsburgh existed, this creek valley was a major travel corridor for Native Americans and European explorers alike!

name: Robert McAfee Bridge
funFact: Brighton Heights is one of Pittsburgh's largest North Side hilltop neighborhoods, perched above the Ohio River valley with sweeping views — the McAfee family were among the earliest settlers of western Pennsylvania in the late 1700s, and this bridge honors that pioneer legacy!

name: Shadeland Avenue Bridge
funFact: Marshall-Shadeland sits along the Ohio River on Pittsburgh's North Side and was once home to major industrial facilities along the waterfront — today the neighborhood is known for its riverfront trails and scenic Ohio River Boulevard that draws cyclists and walkers from across the city!

name: Commercial Street Bridge
funFact: The CSX rail corridor under this bridge was once part of the Pennsylvania Railroad, one of the most powerful companies in American history — at its peak the 'Pennsy' was the largest railroad in the world and employed more workers than any other U.S. company!

name: Jacks Run Bridge
funFact: Jacks Run Creek carved one of the North Side's many hidden valleys as it flows toward the Ohio River — the run was named after an early settler who built a cabin along its banks in the 1700s, long before Pittsburgh's neighborhoods spread across these hills!

name: Eckert Street Bridge
funFact: Brighton Heights sits on one of Pittsburgh's highest North Side ridges, and the hollows and creek valleys below it — like the Jacks Run valley this bridge crosses — were carved by glacial meltwater thousands of years ago, giving the neighborhood its dramatic hilltop character!

name: Charles J. Lieberth Pedestrian Walkway
funFact: The Strip District gets its name from the narrow strip of flat land squeezed between Pittsburgh's hills and the Allegheny River — this pedestrian walkway gives walkers a safe crossing over the busy railroad corridors that made the Strip one of America's great wholesale food markets for over a century!

name: Veterans Bridge
funFact: Veterans Bridge honors Pittsburgh's long tradition of military service — Pittsburgh was a crucial supply and manufacturing hub in both World Wars, producing steel, armor, and weapons that helped the Allied forces win, earning the city the nickname 'Arsenal of the World'!

name: Wind Gap Bridge
funFact: A 'wind gap' is a geographic term for a dry valley carved by an ancient stream that has since changed course — the Wind Gap area in Pittsburgh's West End sits where Chartiers Creek once cut a new path, leaving behind a notch in the ridge that early travelers used as a natural passage through the hills!

name: Ansonia Place Bridge
funFact: Knoxville sits on one of Pittsburgh's highest South Hills ridges and was named for General Henry Knox, the Revolutionary War general who also served as the first U.S. Secretary of War under President George Washington!

name: Calera Street Bridge 1
funFact: Hays is one of Pittsburgh's most isolated neighborhoods, tucked into the hills south of the Monongahela River — the area was once home to major steel facilities along the riverbank, and the neighborhood's remote hilltop location gave steel workers a quiet escape from the industrial valley below!

name: Calera Street Bridge 2
funFact: Glen Hazel sits adjacent to the massive 644-acre Frick Park, and residents here are just a short walk from some of Pittsburgh's wildest urban forest trails — Frick Park is one of the largest municipally-owned natural parks in any American city!

name: Ramp Street Bridge
funFact: The South Side Slopes is one of Pittsburgh's most unique neighborhoods, where some streets are so steep they were named 'Ramp' — this is one of the few American neighborhoods where GPS navigation regularly fails because the streets, steps, and ramps form a three-dimensional maze on the hillside!

name: Swindell Bridge
funFact: The Swindell Bridge is one of Pittsburgh's oldest surviving metal bridges — built in 1894 and named after iron manufacturer William Swindell, it still carries pedestrians over the railroad tracks on the North Side and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places!

name: Bloomfield Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: This pedestrian bridge connects Bloomfield — Pittsburgh's 'Little Italy' — to the neighborhoods south of the railroad corridor, giving residents a safe crossing over the same tracks that once carried steel and coal produced by the Italian immigrant workforce who built this community!

name: East Liberty Boulevard Bridge
funFact: East Liberty Boulevard runs through one of Pittsburgh's most transformed neighborhoods — where department stores and theaters once stood, you'll now find Google's Pittsburgh office, a thriving restaurant scene, and one of the city's most walkable and bikeable commercial corridors!

name: Hot Metal Street Bridge
funFact: Hot Metal Street gets its name from the 'hot metal' transfer cars that once hauled molten iron from the blast furnaces on the South Side to the steel mills — those glowing ladles of liquid iron traveling through the streets at night made for one of Pittsburgh's most spectacular industrial sights!

name: South 12th Street Bridge
funFact: The South Side Flats numbered street grid runs from 1st to 22nd Street along the Monongahela River — this orderly layout was unusual for 19th-century Pittsburgh and made the South Side one of the easiest neighborhoods to navigate in a city famous for its confusing streets and hills!

name: Panther Hollow Road Bridge
funFact: Panther Hollow Road winds through one of Schenley Park's most scenic ravines, where Panther Hollow Run flows through a surprisingly wild gorge right in the middle of the city — early settlers reported seeing mountain lions in this hollow, which is how it got its dramatic name!

name: Reservoir Drive Bridge
funFact: Reservoir Drive circles the Highland Park Reservoir, one of Pittsburgh's historic water storage facilities — the reservoir and its surrounding park were designed in the 1870s by famed landscape architect William Falconer, who trained under Frederick Law Olmsted, the designer of New York's Central Park!

name: Nine Mile Run Trail Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: Nine Mile Run is one of Pittsburgh's longest urban streams and was severely polluted from decades of steel industry waste — a massive cleanup and restoration project transformed it into a thriving natural waterway, making it one of the largest urban stream restoration projects in American history!

name: Herr's Island Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: Herr's Island was once one of Pittsburgh's most polluted industrial sites, used as a livestock stockyard and slaughterhouse — after a massive cleanup it became Washington's Landing, a model urban redevelopment with condos, offices, and riverfront trails that transformed the island entirely!

name: Timberland Avenue Bridge
funFact: Beechview's tree-lined streets were named for the beech trees that once covered Pittsburgh's South Hills, and the neighborhood still has some of the city's most beautiful autumn foliage — the steep hills and ravines that required bridges like this one also helped preserve the natural landscape from development!

name: Overbrook Way Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: Overbrook is one of Pittsburgh's South Hills neighborhoods perched above Saw Mill Run, and the steep hollows here required pedestrian bridges so residents could reach neighbors just a short distance away — Pittsburgh's terrain means a 5-minute walk can sometimes feel like a mountain hike!

name: Edgebrook Avenue Bridge
funFact: Saw Mill Run Creek flows through the valley below Overbrook and Beechview, and the mills that once lined its banks gave the creek its name — by the late 1800s, Pittsburgh's streams powered dozens of sawmills, gristmills, and ironworks that transformed raw Pennsylvania wilderness into a booming industrial city!

name: Eads Street Bridge
funFact: Hazelwood Green, the massive former LTV Steel plant site nearby, is being redeveloped into one of America's most ambitious urban innovation campuses — the 178-acre project is transforming one of Pittsburgh's old industrial scars into a hub for research, green technology, and community space!

name: Englewood Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: Carrick and Overbrook sit on Pittsburgh's far South Hills, and their winding streets and deep hollows made pedestrian bridges essential for getting around — in these neighborhoods, a bridge might be the only way to visit a neighbor who lives just 100 feet away on the other side of a ravine!

name: Schenley Park Panther Hollow Trail Bridge
funFact: Panther Hollow Run flows through one of Schenley Park's most beautiful ravines, and the trail bridges here let hikers explore a surprisingly wild gorge just minutes from the University of Pittsburgh and Carnegie Mellon — Pittsburgh's parks feel like wilderness escapes hidden right inside the city!

name: Finland Street Pedestrian Bridge
funFact: Finland Street is one of many Bloomfield streets named after European countries, reflecting the diverse immigrant heritage of Pittsburgh's 'Little Italy' neighborhood — alongside the Italian community, families from Poland, Hungary, and many other nations made Bloomfield one of Pittsburgh's most culturally rich communities!
